revision.text:
[[Kasama]] Inari Shrine or `Kasama Jinja` is one of the three largest [[Inari Okami]] shrines in Japan, having been awarded the ancient court rank of Senior First Grade. According to legends associated with the shrine, it was founded in 651 during the reign of [[Emperor Kotoku]], indicating a history extending over some thirteen centuries.<ref name="Kasama Inari Shrine"> {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | work=The Great Kami Inari and Kasama Inari Shrine | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} </ref>
During the Tokugawa or [[Edo Period]], Kasama Inari Shrine received the devoted patronage of the feudal lord of the Kasama Domain, and spread its influence not only through the Kanto region but throughout all of Japan. At present, the shrine is visited by more than 3.5 million pilgrims each year.<ref name="Kasama Inari Shrine"> {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | work=The Great Kami Inari and Kasama Inari Shrine | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} </ref>
The shrine is dedicated to Ukanomitama no kami - a spirit with jurisdiction over the five grains and foodstuffs, the life-root kami having mastery over the sources of life itself. According to the oldest collection of Japanese mythology, the [[Kojiki]] ("Records of Ancient Matters"), ''Ukanomitama no kami'' was the child of ''Susano no okami'' and ''Kamuoichihime no kami''. The "Uka" in the name means foodstuffs, indicating the "mysterious spirit dwelling in the grain."<ref name="Kasama Inari Shrine"> {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | work=The Great Kami Inari and Kasama Inari Shrine | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} </ref>
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index.html/ Kasama Inari Jinja - English] Official Website of Kasama Inari Jinja * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honcho - English] - The Official Japanese Organization of 80,000 Shinto Shrines
---- {{Religion topics}}
[[Category:Folk religions]] [[Category:Japanese religions terms]] [[Category:Religion in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto| ]] [[Category:East Asian religions]]

309-1611 Japan | phone = +81-0296-73-0001 | website = http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/ English }} {{Shintoism}} [[Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama]] Inari Shrine ({{lang|ja|笠間稲荷神社}} {{transl|ja|Kasama Jinja}}) is one of the three largest [[Inari Okami]] shrines in Japan, having been awarded the ancient court rank of Senior First Grade. According to legends associated with the shrine, it was founded in 651 during the reign of [[Emperor Kotoku]], indicating a history extending over some thirteen centuries. {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | work=The Great Kami Inari and Kasama Inari Shrine | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} During the Tokugawa or [[Edo period]], Kasama Inari Shrine received the devoted patronage of the feudal lord of the Kasama Domain, and spread its influence not only through the [[Kantō region]] but throughout all of Japan. At present, the shrine is visited by more than 3.5 million pilgrims each year. {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | work=The Great Kami Inari and Kasama Inari Shrine | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} The shrine is dedicated to [[Ukanomitama]] no kami – a spirit with jurisdiction over the five grains and foodstuffs, the life-root kami having mastery over the sources of life itself. According to the oldest collection of Japanese mythology, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' ("Records of Ancient Matters"), ''Ukanomitama no kami'' was the child of ''Susano no okami'' and ''Kamuoichihime no kami''. The "Uka" in the name means foodstuffs, indicating the "mysterious spirit dwelling in the grain". {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | work=The Great Kami Inari and Kasama Inari Shrine | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Kasama Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honcho – English] – The Official Japanese Organization of 80,000 Shinto Shrines ---- {{Religion topics}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]]

According to legends associated with the shrine, it was founded in 651 during the reign of [[Emperor Kotoku]], indicating a history extending over some thirteen centuries. {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | work=The Great Kami Inari and Kasama Inari Shrine | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} During the Tokugawa or [[Edo period]], Kasama Inari Shrine received the devoted patronage of the feudal lord of the Kasama Domain, and spread its influence not only through the [[Kantō region]] but throughout all of Japan. At present, the shrine is visited by more than 3.5 million pilgrims each year. The shrine is dedicated to [[Ukanomitama]] no kami – a spirit with jurisdiction over the five grains and foodstuffs, the life-root kami having mastery over the sources of life itself. According to the oldest collection of Japanese mythology, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' ("Records of Ancient Matters"), ''Ukanomitama no kami'' was the child of ''Susano no okami'' and ''Kamuoichihime no kami''. The "Uka" in the name means foodstuffs, indicating the "mysterious spirit dwelling in the grain". ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kasama Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honcho – English] – The Official Japanese Organization of 80,000 Shinto Shrines ---- {{Religion topics}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1860]] [[Category:Kasama, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Beppo Shrines]]

309-1611 Japan | website = {{URL|http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index.html}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Shintoism}} '''[[Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama]] Inari Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|笠間稲荷神社}} {{transl|ja|Kasama Jinja}}) is one of the three largest [[Inari Okami]] shrines in Japan, having been awarded the ancient court rank of Senior First Grade. According to legends associated with the shrine, it was founded in 651 during the reign of [[Emperor Kotoku]], indicating a history extending over some thirteen centuries. {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} During the Tokugawa or [[Edo period]], Kasama Inari Shrine received the devoted patronage of the feudal lord of the Kasama Domain, and spread its influence not only through the [[Kantō region]] but throughout all of Japan. At present, the shrine is visited by more than 3.5 million pilgrims each year. The shrine is dedicated to [[Ukanomitama]] no kami – a spirit with jurisdiction over the five grains and foodstuffs, the life-root kami having mastery over the sources of life itself. According to the oldest collection of Japanese mythology, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' ("Records of Ancient Matters"), ''Ukanomitama no kami'' was the child of ''Susano no okami'' and ''Kamuoichihime no kami''. The "Uka" in the name means foodstuffs, indicating the "mysterious spirit dwelling in the grain". ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kasama Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honcho – English] – The Official Japanese Organization of 80,000 Shinto Shrines ---- {{Religion topics}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1860]] [[Category:Kasama, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Ibaraki Prefecture]] {{Inari Faith}}

According to legends associated with the shrine, it was founded in 651 during the reign of [[Emperor Kotoku]], indicating a history extending over some thirteen centuries. {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} During the Tokugawa or [[Edo period]], Kasama Inari Shrine received the devoted patronage of the feudal lord of the Kasama Domain, and spread its influence not only through the [[Kantō region]] but throughout all of Japan. At present, the shrine is visited by more than 3.5 million pilgrims each year. The shrine is dedicated to [[Ukanomitama]] no kami – a spirit with jurisdiction over the five grains and foodstuffs, the life-root kami having mastery over the sources of life itself. According to the oldest collection of Japanese mythology, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' ("Records of Ancient Matters"), ''Ukanomitama no kami'' was the child of ''Susano no okami'' and ''Kamuoichihime no kami''. The "Uka" in the name means foodstuffs, indicating the "mysterious spirit dwelling in the grain". ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kasama Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honcho – English] – The Official Japanese Organization of 80,000 Shinto Shrines ---- {{Religion topics}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1860]] [[Category:Kasama, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:ginality]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category: 5]]\[[Category:Completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category: 5]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Kasama Inari Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:New]]

笠間稲荷神社 | image = Kasama-RD-2Feb2010.JPG | caption = New Years crowd at Kasama Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Inari Shrine]] | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami]] as
[[Inari Ōkami]] | founded_by = | established = 651 | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Kasama, [[Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama-shi]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki-ken]]
309-1611 Japan | website = {{URL|http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index.html}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Shintoism}} '''[[Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama]] Inari Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|笠間稲荷神社}} {{transl|ja|Kasama Jinja}}) is one of the three largest [[Inari Okami]] shrines in Japan, having been awarded the ancient court rank of Senior First Grade. According to legends associated with the shrine, it was founded in 651 during the reign of [[Emperor Kotoku]], indicating a history extending over some thirteen centuries. {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} During the Tokugawa or [[Edo period]], Kasama Inari Shrine received the devoted patronage of the feudal lord of the Kasama Domain, and spread its influence not only through the [[Kantō region]] but throughout all of Japan. At present, the shrine is visited by more than 3.5 million pilgrims each year. The shrine is dedicated to [[Ukanomitama]] no kami – a spirit with jurisdiction over the five grains and foodstuffs, the life-root kami having mastery over the sources of life itself. According to the oldest collection of Japanese mythology, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' ("Records of Ancient Matters"), ''Ukanomitama no kami'' was the child of ''Susano no okami'' and ''Kamuoichihime no kami''. The "Uka" in the name means foodstuffs, indicating the "mysterious spirit dwelling in the grain". ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kasama Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honcho – English] – The Official Japanese Organization of 80,000 Shinto Shrines ---- {{Religion topics}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1860]] [[Category:Kasama, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:ginality]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: tier 6]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category: 5]]\[[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: tier 6]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category: 5]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Kasama Inari Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Kasama Inari Shrine]][[Category:New]]

The shrine is dedicated to [[Ukanomitama]] no kami – a spirit with jurisdiction over the five grains and foodstuffs, the life-root kami having mastery over the sources of life itself. According to the oldest collection of Japanese mythology, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' ("Records of Ancient Matters"), ''Ukanomitama no kami'' was the child of ''Susano no okami'' and ''Kamuoichihime no kami''. The "Uka" in the name means foodstuffs, indicating the "mysterious spirit dwelling in the grain". ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kasama Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honcho – English] – The Official Japanese Organization of 80,000 Shinto Shrines ---- {{Religion topics}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1860]] [[Category:Kasama, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Ibaraki Prefecture]] }} [[sn:Kasama Inari Shrine]] [[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[eo:Sanktejo Kasama Inari]] [[es:Santuario Kasama Inari]] [[eu:Kasama Inari santutegia]] [[fr:Kasama Inari-jinja]] [[ja:笠間稲荷神社]] [[zh:笠間稻荷神社]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]] | founded_by = | established = 651 | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Kasama, [[:en:Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama-shi]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki-ken]]
309-1611 Japan | website = {{URL|http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index.html}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Shintoism}} '''[[:en:Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama]] Inari Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|笠間稲荷神社}} {{transl|ja|Kasama Jinja}}) is one of the three largest [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari Ōkami]] shrines in Japan, having been awarded the ancient court Rank of Senior First Grade. According to legends associated with the shrine, it was founded in 651 during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kotoku|Emperor Kotoku]], indicating a history extending over some thirteen centuries. {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} During the Tokugawa or [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Kasama Inari Shrine received the devoted patronage of the feudal lord of the Kasama Domain, and spread its influence not only through the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] but throughout all of Japan. At present, the shrine is visited by more than 3.5 million pilgrims each year. The shrine is dedicated to [[Ukanomitama]] no kami – a spirit with jurisdiction over the five grains and foodstuffs, the life-root kami having mastery over the sources of life itself. According to the oldest collection of Japanese mythology, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' ("Records of Ancient Matters"), ''Ukanomitama no kami'' was the child of ''Susano no okami'' and ''Kamuoichihime no kami''. The "Uka" in the name means foodstuffs, indicating the "mysterious spirit dwelling in the grain". ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kasama Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honcho – English] – The Official Japanese Organization of 80,000 Shinto Shrines ---- {{Religion topics}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1860]] [[Category:Kasama, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Ibaraki Prefecture]] }} [[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[eo:Sanktejo Kasama Inari]] [[es:Santuario Kasama Inari]] [[eu:Kasama Inari santutegia]] [[fr:Kasama Inari-jinja]] [[ja:笠間稲荷神社]] [[zh:笠間稻荷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q704876}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The "Uka" in the name means foodstuffs, indicating the "mysterious spirit dwelling in the grain". ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kasama Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honcho – English] – The Official Japanese Organization of 80,000 Shinto Shrines ---- {{Religion topics}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} [[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]] [[eo:Sanktejo Kasama Inari]] [[es:Santuario Kasama Inari]] [[eu:Kasama Inari santutegia]] [[fr:Kasama Inari-jinja]] [[ja:笠間稲荷神社]] [[zh:笠間稻荷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q704876}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Kasama, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1860]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

According to legends associated with the shrine, it was founded in 651 during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kotoku|Emperor Kotoku]], indicating a history extending over some thirteen centuries. {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} During the Tokugawa or [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Kasama Inari Shrine received the devoted patronage of the feudal lord of the Kasama Domain, and spread its influence not only through the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] but throughout all of Japan. At present, the shrine is visited by more than 3.5 million pilgrims each year. The shrine is dedicated to [[Ukanomitama]] no kami – a spirit with jurisdiction over the five grains and foodstuffs, the life-root kami having mastery over the sources of life itself. According to the oldest collection of Japanese mythology, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' ("Records of Ancient Matters"), ''Ukanomitama no kami'' was the child of ''Susano no okami'' and ''Kamuoichihime no kami''. The "Uka" in the name means foodstuffs, indicating the "mysterious spirit dwelling in the grain". ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kasama Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honcho – English] – The Official Japanese Organization of 80,000 Shinto Shrines ---- {{Religion topics}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} [[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]] [[eo:Sanktejo Kasama Inari]] [[es:Santuario Kasama Inari]] [[eu:Kasama Inari santutegia]] [[fr:Kasama Inari-jinja]] [[ja:笠間稲荷神社]] [[zh:笠間稻荷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q704876}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Kasama, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1860]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The shrine is dedicated to [[Ukanomitama]] no kami – a spirit with jurisdiction over the five grains and foodstuffs, the life-root kami having mastery over the sources of life itself. According to the oldest collection of Japanese mythology, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' ("Records of Ancient Matters"), ''Ukanomitama no kami'' was the child of ''Susano no okami'' and ''Kamuoichihime no kami''. The "Uka" in the name means foodstuffs, indicating the "mysterious spirit dwelling in the grain". ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kasama Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honcho – English] – The Official Japanese Organization of 80,000 Shinto Shrines ---- {{Religion topics}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]] [[eo:Sanktejo Kasama Inari]] [[es:Santuario Kasama Inari]] [[eu:Kasama Inari santutegia]] [[fr:Kasama Inari-jinja]] [[ja:笠間稲荷神社]] [[zh:笠間稻荷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q704876}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Kasama, Ibaraki]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1860]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

309-1611 Japan | website = {{URL|http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index.html}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Shintoism}} '''{{ill|Kasama, Ibaraki|en|Kasama, Ibaraki|WD=Q845851|lt=Kasama}} Inari Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|笠間稲荷神社}} {{transl|ja|Kasama Jinja}}) is one of the three largest {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami}} shrines in Japan, having been awarded the ancient court Rank of Senior First Grade. According to legends associated with the shrine, it was founded in 651 during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kotoku|Emperor Kotoku]], indicating a history extending over some thirteen centuries. {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} During the Tokugawa or {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Kasama Inari Shrine received the devoted patronage of the feudal lord of the Kasama Domain, and spread its influence not only through the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} but throughout all of Japan. At present, the shrine is visited by more than 3.5 million pilgrims each year. The shrine is dedicated to [[Ukanomitama]] no kami – a spirit with jurisdiction over the five grains and foodstuffs, the life-root kami having mastery over the sources of life itself. According to the oldest collection of Japanese mythology, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' ("Records of Ancient Matters"), ''Ukanomitama no kami'' was the child of ''Susano no okami'' and ''Kamuoichihime no kami''. The "Uka" in the name means foodstuffs, indicating the "mysterious spirit dwelling in the grain". ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kasama Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honcho – English] – The Official Japanese Organization of 80,000 Shinto Shrines ---- {{Religion topics}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]] [[eo:Sanktejo Kasama Inari]] [[es:Santuario Kasama Inari]] [[eu:Kasama Inari santutegia]] [[fr:Kasama Inari-jinja]] [[ja:笠間稲荷神社]] [[zh:笠間稻荷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q704876}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Kasama, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1860]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

{{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}} | founded_by = | established = 651 | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Kasama, {{ill|Kasama, Ibaraki|en|Kasama, Ibaraki|WD=Q845851|lt=Kasama-shi|1=Kasama, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki-ken|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}
309-1611 Japan | website = {{URL|http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index.html}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Shintoism}} '''{{ill|Kasama, Ibaraki|en|Kasama, Ibaraki|WD=Q845851|lt=Kasama|1=Kasama, Ibaraki}} Inari Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|笠間稲荷神社}} {{transl|ja|Kasama Jinja}}) is one of the three largest {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}} shrines in Japan, having been awarded the ancient court Rank of Senior First Grade. According to legends associated with the shrine, it was founded in 651 during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kotoku|Emperor Kotoku]], indicating a history extending over some thirteen centuries. {{cite web | publisher=Shrine Office of Kasama Inari Jinja | url=http://www.kasama.or.jp/english/index3.html | title=The Great Kami Inari & Kasama Inari Shrine | accessdate=2010-09-05 }} During the Tokugawa or {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Kasama Inari Shrine received the devoted patronage of the feudal lord of the Kasama Domain, and spread its influence not only through the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} but throughout all of Japan. At present, the shrine is visited by more than 3.5 million pilgrims each year. The shrine is dedicated to [[Ukanomitama]] no kami – a spirit with jurisdiction over the five grains and foodstuffs, the life-root kami having mastery over the sources of life itself. According to the oldest collection of Japanese mythology, the ''[[Kojiki]]'' ("Records of Ancient Matters"), ''Ukanomitama no kami'' was the child of ''Susano no okami'' and ''Kamuoichihime no kami''. The "Uka" in the name means foodstuffs, indicating the "mysterious spirit dwelling in the grain". ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kasama Inari-jinja}} * [http://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/ Jinja Honcho – English] – The Official Japanese Organization of 80,000 Shinto Shrines ---- {{Religion topics}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Kasama Inari Shrine]] [[eo:Sanktejo Kasama Inari]] [[es:Santuario Kasama Inari]] [[eu:Kasama Inari santutegia]] [[fr:Kasama Inari-jinja]] [[ja:笠間稲荷神社]] [[zh:笠間稻荷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q704876}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Kasama, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1860]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
